item.title: Musings 5: Mr. Rogers
item.description:
• I came across this video today.

  • Makes me want to reflect on my previous articles that I have written for school, and how inflammatory they could've been. The content of the writings weren't wrong, but having done some reflection, I don't think I have persuaded anyone to be on my side. It's truly a lesson to be learned, and I hope I can improve on this.

item.title: Musings 4: North Korean and UN Sanctions
item.description:
• The United Nations Security Council (including China) voted to pass on new sanctions for North Korea yesterday in the midst of this nuclear missle craziness. While the sanctions that dent the already poor North Korean economy sounds nice, I'm skeptical of it. I'm afraid this would hurt the poor and oppressed norNorth Korean civilians, than the true criminal in position, Kim Jung Un in any capacity.

    • Historically speaking, sanctions are nothing new for the North. They've dealt with it since the nation's inception. It's called a hermit kingdom for a reason. They mostly rely on their own land to support itself, of course with exceptions being the trades with fellow dictator enabling nation, China. The natural corollary of that is if something were to happen to its essential natural resources (i.e. rice), the nation is in trouble. This is what happened during the 90's famine and resulted in possbiliy more than 3 million deaths. To save the North Koreans from starving themselves to death, what followed was the weasel trade policy with the South called, Sunshine Policy. And to no one's surprise, during all this time of economic hardship, Kim Jung Il was perfectly fine and plump, living off of the little resources North Koreans scrapped for him. The people took the biggest hit. Not the communist regime.

    North Korea is facing the famine once again, this time under the leadership of even (to put it nicely) nuttier son, Kim Jung Un. This is bad news for the already starving people of North Korea. They have a dictator who doesn't care if the civilians starve or not and instead using up the only resources they have on building internalcontinental ballistic missiles. I'm afraid the recent UN sanctions will make the matter worse for just the North Korean people. The only fat kid in the entire nation will be fine in his palace, building his toy missiles.

    So what is the solution? My proposal is pretty simple. Removal of the leadership in the fastest and the swiftest way possible with the support of China. In terms of the actual assasination, there is no reason the SEAL team can handle this. Removal of Kim Jung Un is the only road to peace and liberation of the dying North Koreans. Say what you will about people dying in the processs, but pacifism does not lead to peace (even Peter Singer said so!). The United States has the world's most highly trained troops sitting in standby for this purpose. But the key to a swift, forceful removal of the communist regime lies in the blessing from China. China is the only ally of North Korea since the inception of the nation in 1948. It has been the truly bad actor in enabling the Kim dynasty. If Xi Jingping wants to portray the image that they don't want nulcear proliferation as he does these days, then they he must truly act on it. Xi Jingping and the rest of the Chinese Communist party's contribution of to North Korea is undeniable. And these days, they are pretending as if they don't want nuclear proliferation and continued rise of the North. If that is truly so, they must help the U.S. (if we were to take on this global responsibility), fix this problem. For those triggered, this is not a call for nation building, imperialism, or petty war with economic gain in sight. This is a humanitarian effort that must be pursued before more North Koreans starve to death. And there is only one nation that stands in our way, and that is the sole ally China. Unless we have their approval, the people of North Korea will continue to live and die in the fear of the Big Brother.

item.title: Musings 3: Diversity
item.description:
• When it comes to today's colleges, their love fo "fostering diversity" is truly deceitful and misleading. We should call them for what they truly are. Diversity in college is simply a form of reverse racism, where you value/devalue students based on the color of their skin (or gender). Asian students benefit the least in terms of college entrance. Blacks and Hispanics benefit the most. It's affirmative action based on your phenotype, and this would be the very type of bias, whether well-intentioned or not, Dr. Martin Luther King surely would have been against. What did Dr. King say? "I have a dream that my four little children will one day live in a nation where they will not be judged by the color of their skin, but by the content of their character." He would be turning in his grave, if he saw what was going on in college campuses' crazy wave of intersectionality and pseudo-diversity. Call a spade a spade. Diversity on campus is an action based on race, and it fits the very description of racism. Try diversity of thought for a change.

  • Maajid Nawaz has famously coined the phrase "bigotry of low expectations." I think the same could be applied in this situation. If you think black or Hispanic students should be treated differently (again identifying with the color of their skins) because they typically perform worse than white and Asian students on standardized tests (or general qualification for college acceptance), this is a form of bigotted low expectation. If you want black and Hispanic students should perform better, we must talk about cultural differences in emphasis in education.

  • If a form of affirmative action must stay, I fully support an alternate solution based purely on financial background (for college entrance for students). Kids are dependent on their families for support, and finanical stability of the parents is simply a luck of the dice. Kids are of low-income background are often not given opportunities/exposures to better education or experiences in life. Additionally, none of this based on the color of your phenotype.

  • In this very well written article by professor of philosophy, Paul Russell at University of British Columbia, he makes a distinction between religious identity and human identity (which are phenotypes such as race, gender, sexual orientation that you have no control over).

    • This is highly relevant to the above point and is a must-read. It all goes in hand with the theme of regressive left intersectionality madness.

  • I truly believe that we have made so much progress in social acceptance in the last couple decades, and we've been spoiled, living amongst the world's priviliged top 1% (that would be America, in case it wasn't clear), that we are becoming blind to the order of priority of human rights. As described by Russell's paper, we find criticsim of religions to be offensive hate speech, while the theocrats themselves advocate for oppression and practice hate. As Dave Rubin once said, we have become so tolerant that we have become tolerant of the intolerant. Hopefully, I can write more on this topic soon.

item.title: Musings 2: Turing Tests
item.description:
• The world isn't beautiful or rosy. We trick ourselves to think it's beautiful to live on.

  • Can machines question their own existensialism? If a script is written to explicitly question their consciousness, is it really conscious? In preparation for the Turing test questions such as, "Are you human?", if a machine is explictly programmed to answer intelligently, in the eyes of the arbiter, how is this not a pass? Humans are deterministic exactly like machines. We just happen to be made of cells instead of plastic, and neurons have larger memory capacity than any man-made chips/storage so far.

  • If we are part of a simulation a la Nick Bostrom, is there a Turing test version for humans that is analogous to man-made machines? What sort or a level of intelligence test are we supposed to test in the eyes of the simulator (creation of the simulation)?

  • If humans can code explictly for machine to fail the Turing test, which is easily achieveable without any effort with the current state of artificial intelligence, it's possible humans are failing some version of Turing test at the moment in the simulation world. It's possible to hard-code some machine so that it doesn't ask certain questions, thus intentionally failing certain tests. Similar argument could be made on humans in the simulation world. Humans may have been hard-wired to evade certain existential questions or to fail higher-dimensional Turing test, all in the context of simulation/simulator.

item.title: Musings 1: Theistic Reasoning vs. Scientific Reasoning
item.description:
• Suppose you're teaching a math class, and you give a problem for students to solve. Students are expected to show all work for their calculations. In solving the problem, one student demonstrates lack of rudimentary understanding of algebra. There are endless number of mistakes in his work, and yet somehow he concludes with the correct answer. Another student demonstrates his thorough foundational knowledge in algebra by carefully showing work line by line, but a mistake in computation costs him the answer. Whose work should be credited as valid? In academia, there wouldn't be a question as to whose work should be accepted as true or not. This is the difference between theisitic reasoning vs. scientific reasoning. Of course I'm not saying that the theists even have the conclusion correct. So technically, thei work is wrong and the final solution most likely would be wrong as well.

  • I'm hesistant with acitivism in general. I worry about the slightest chance that I choose the wrong side of the history. Conviction should be hard to come by. A thorough examination of facts and conclusive choice of action are no trivial tasks. I also make sure no personal or emotional biases are involved in decision making for the very same reason from earlier. That's why it's hard to watch impassioned young students, whose sincerety I don't doubt, run around in mass-hysteria politcal activism. Mass hysteria is a real social phenomenon.

  • Postmodernism contributes to conflation of emotions with facts, and thus makes the politcal activism more temperamental than it was originally intended to be.

item.title: Topics that I will talk about on this blog
item.description:
These are philosophical topics that interest me, and that have nothing to do with my research:

  • Peter Singer's Drowning Child Argument in Wars
  • Cognitive Dissonance
  • Jouranlism vs. Activism (Case Study: Regressive Left)
  • Simulation Hypothesis of Nick Bostrom, and Martin Savage's Setup
  • Ontological Proofs
  • Mortality of my cat, Baxter
